Welcome to session number three of GKIC's flagship program, Dan Kennedy's Magnetic Marketing System. This is the last and possibly most valuable session of all, where Dan describes in detail the strategic thinking behind his world-famous three-step campaign known as the Giorgio Letters. In addition, he'll walk you through a number of proven sales and copywriting secrets guaranteed to put more money in your pocket. Get ready as more leads and greater profits await you. GKIC is now pleased to bring you Dan Kennedy with a third and final session of Magnetic Marketing. The media I want almost every business to use for Magnetic Marketing is their own direct mail. In just about any and every case I can think of, I would use direct mail and I would use the three-step letter sequences before I would use anything else. I would concentrate my resources there. This is the most controllable, trackable, changeable tool that you have. You can do a mailing this week, you can know what the results are next week. If they were good, you may want to increase that mailing and do it again the following week. If they weren't so good, you can change one variable, change two variables, and do another small unit test. You can test these kinds of letters for local businesses in fairly small quantities, 200 units, 300 units, and get a feel for how well they're going to work. There's just about no other method of localized target marketing that you can test for as little money and get the refinement done for as little money. As I go into this example, understand that direct mail is not the only thing I teach, not the only thing you should use, and not the only thing in my system, in fact, far from it. But to be truly in control of your own destiny, you want to own a stable of reliable direct mail campaigns based on the three-step model. As an example, a financial advisor I work with in Pennsylvania has a three-step campaign that fills his introductory workshops, a three-step campaign to generate leads, requesting a free report, a three-step follow-up campaign to people who attend the workshops but fail to immediately book appointments, all used consistently seven to ten times a year. These have fueled his million-dollar-a-year business with nominal changes for at least five years. A dentist with four offices has three-step letter campaigns built right out of the magnetic marketing system for new movers, for geodemographic neighborhoods, for leads obtained initially from online and social media, for appointment no sale when he makes a case presentation to a patient and does not get immediate acceptance, and for lost inactive patients. These are responsible for about 50% of his practice's revenue, and they have been locked down and used with virtually no changes for almost seven years. It is nearly impossible to get that kind of long-term use out of advertising done in online, print, or broadcast media. Still, as soon as I start to talk about doing your own direct mail, I hear we've tried direct mail. It doesn't work in our state, city, business, industry, product category. It's too expensive. It's too complicated. A couple things about that. First of all, if you and I go through life only doing the things that work real, real well for us the very first time we do them, there will be less kids with each generation. Second, you have not tried direct mail the way I'm going to show you how to do direct mail. In fact, almost everybody makes this mistake when they do try it. They get a list from somewhere, maybe not a very good list, but they get a list. They print up something, they send it to the list, and almost no matter what happens, ugly, bad, good, they never mail to those same people again. Or if they do, it's three, six, eight months later by accident, and they send them the same stuff, they sent them the first time. Save your money. Never do one shot mail. Let me explain to you why that can't work. Here's how people do not live. The last thing Harvey says to Marge when he leaves the house is not Marge, I want you to stay home. Don't go to work. Don't take the kids to school. Don't go to the grocery store. Do not leave this house. Stay right here in the foyer with your nose pressed against the window watching for the mailman. When the mailman arrives, I want you to hustle your buns out to the mailbox and get the mail before somebody gets it. A bird snatches it. It catches on fire or it gets wet. Get it back in the house. Guard it until I get home. Together, we will open the mail because today might be the day that a life insurance agent sends us one of those nifty letters where we can get a free Starbucks gift card if we let them come over to our house and beat our brains in for three hours and I want that Starbucks card. This is not how people live their lives. What makes people think they can print up one crummy golden rod flyer or a big postcard, send it out one time and get some stampede of response. It's a mystery to me. The thing doesn't even nick them on the way by. They don't even feel the breeze. So the example I'm going to cover here is a different thing entirely. So pay close attention as there are a lot of lessons here that you can apply not only to direct mail but to other forms of media as well. The example I'm going to show you comes from the restaurant industry. I use it for two reasons. One, if you had a room full of restaurant owners, they'd all swear it won't work. If they do mail or use Valpak, they do simple, basic look-alike offers typically discount and cheap price driven. Two dinners for one price with free dessert. That's their only idea of a marketing message. This example shows how you can take your marketing message, deliver it to a chosen market effectively, efficiently and affordably, and magnetically bring ready to buy customers and clients and dominate a market in 45 days or less. In a controlled test 25 years ago from this campaign mailed to 2,000 households, a restaurant booked about 140 dinners. Since then, literally thousands of restaurants have used this mailing or one adapted from it and enjoyed 2%, 5%, even 7% response acquiring new first-time customers. I doubt seriously if you could find any other approach to advertising and marketing that would get those kind of results for restaurants. Now you can look at the Giorgio letters in your toolkit. Essentially, I invented a new business within the business. I hope that you can see that. I invented the romance business and plugged it into the restaurant business and dramatically changed the marketing message. Letter number one goes to a geodemographically selected list for an Italian restaurant. In real life where the square is, there's a photograph of the owner of the restaurant. The headline says, a confidential letter to the husband of the house from Giorgio, the romance director of Giorgio's Italian grotto. I'll read you just two of the paragraphs. Dear husband, women are different than we are. Your loving life needs, wants and deserves special attention, maybe more often than you think to give it to her. You are busy, preoccupied with work, aggravated with that dumb-dumb you have to deal with every day at the office, tired, who has the time or the energy to even think about romance. Two-thirds of all marriages end in divorce and the number one reason given by divorcing women, he just didn't pay enough attention to me anymore. I wrote this while I was watching Oprah. You do remember Oprah, don't you? It goes on to present a solution to the problem, which in this case is a prepackaged evening of romance. Table in a special section, five-course meal, strolling violinist, rose in a bud vase, heart-shaped box of candy to take home, souvenir photograph, all for one set price. That's the widget that was created for this sequence. So what happens to everybody that gets this letter and does not respond? 15 days later, they get a second one. This one too has a picture of Giorgio in real life. Where the circles are, there are three pennies glued to the letter. The headline says, three coins in the fountain. The second paragraph says, you see, this is your second notice. Your romance wake-up call from me, Giorgio, the romance director. My belt holds. Does it hold for thee? This letter goes on to restate the problem, restate the solution, remake the offer, and it works and gets response. So what happens to everybody that gets letter number one, letter number two, who still does not respond? 10 days later, they get letter number three. Letter number three has a picture of Giorgio. The headline says, hear that lonesome whipper-will? He sounds too blue to cry. Dear husband, this is Giorgio, too blue to cry, disappointed, attached, or copies of the two letters I previously sent you. And the real stubborn ones that don't respond to that soon get a postcard that says, can this marriage be saved? Do you have any doubt that in any household that gets the three letters, and if necessary the postcard, Giorgio is not the topic of conversation. It doesn't matter who opens the mail. They're showing it to each other. They're showing it to their neighbors. Are you getting this guy's mail? And some are, and some aren't. When Giorgio walks into a 7-11, or into a dry cleaners in the community, people gather around, tell them how much they enjoy getting his mail, when they've got reservations at the restaurant, and ask for his autograph. For the price of three letters and a postcard, he is the dominant presence in his category of business in 45 days or less in his chosen target market. You can't do it more efficiently than that. Now a few people are saying, wait a minute, I sell very sophisticated stuff to very sophisticated people, and I could never do anything like that. It's unprofessional. Well, you can separate style from structure. But I want you to know, this works even better in business to business than it does in consumer, because everything else they get in business to business mail is deadly dull, institutional, and boring. I've used variations on Giorgio in marketing to hospital CEOs, financial advisors, and doctors, keeping the tongue in cheek style and tone. I came up with the structure many years ago, wherein one year I managed to have two cars repossessed and go personally and corporately bankrupt. I got it all over with it once. During that year, I became intimately familiar with the collection industry, and I noticed a pattern. First notice, second notice, third notice, 15 days apart. There's no mystery. They're writing to you repeatedly. It's technically called linkage. Each letter refers to the previous letter. Generally, the last one has copies of everything they sent you before, with final notice rubber stamped all over it, stuffed in the envelope. I saw it over and over and over again. I said, if this will get money from people who haven't got it, offering them nothing, I wonder what would happen if we tried it on people who've got some money and offered them something. It became one of my most reliable magnetic marketing models, and I commend it to you highly for you to try and to apply some determination and persistence to make it work. Owning a campaign like this that you can use repeatedly over a long period of time to acquire customers is a form of business and financial security as good as and maybe even better than money in the bank. A lot of business owners balk at all of this because they are intimidated by the idea of writing sales letters and ads that work like the ones we've been talking about. So I want to give you some methods that will make it easier to do than you might think. The first suggestion is, maybe obviously, use the examples in your toolkit. Borrow, adapt, but stay as true to them as you can. Make your starting point the big idea of your most desirable benefit and instantly communicate that. Usually this is going to impact your very first upfront headline. Headlines and all marketing really revolve around some very basic motives and some very basic techniques. There's a classic headline format that says they didn't think I could fill in the blank, but I did. That comes from a famous ad written by John Capels, who is a legend in the advertising industry. There's even an annual Capels Award for copywriting. The ad sold millions of dollars of homestudy piano lessons and its headline said they laughed when I sat down at the piano until I started to play. This uses something that is very desirable to all of us, the desire to impress the people that we're around, to be an important person. We all want to show off at parties, at work. We want to tell the best joke. We want to have the newest joke. We want to be able to do the trick. We want to be able to pick all the football games right on Friday so that on Monday we can gloat about it with our co-workers. That kind of thing turns us all on. So a headline that says, hey, everybody laughed at me. Nobody thought I could do this. And then I stuck it to them and I did it. That gets folks excited. So you can apply it to just about anything. It was also used for another home study company selling foreign language courses. They grinned when the waiter spoke to me in French, but their laughter changed to amazement at my reply. With this, you're pushing emotional buttons. And that in and of itself is a very important point. Many business owners believe that their business or their product is not subject to an emotional cell. They'll tell me, well, that works fine for some kind of diet plan. You're going to sell in a national inquire. But my business has nothing to do with emotion. All businesses have to do with emotion, because the only way anybody ever makes a decision to respond to anything is on an emotional level. Then we apply logic to make ourselves feel good about what we decided to do in the first place. But we respond first emotionally. And if you want to get favorable attention, you have to operate using emotional buttons. One path to a big idea and a great headline is to figure out the emotional payoff of the benefits of your products or services. There are always functionality benefits and always emotional benefits. New dentures provide functionality benefits. You can eat, chew, enjoy eating and have better health. But the emotional payoff of quote, never being embarrassed about smiling again, are more magnetic. To a business owner, having a well-trained staff has functionality benefits. But the emotional payoff of pride, being constantly complimented about your staff, is more magnetic. Another headline that has broad application and has its own emotional payoff built in is, secrets of blank reveal. If you want to jazz it up further, you stick a number in front of it, 49 secrets, 22 secrets, 72 secrets. Or you make it the secret somebody doesn't want you to know. This works because we all deep down inside believe that there are secrets, that somebody knows some secrets that we don't know. And if we just knew them, that'd make the difference. There is also some ego and pride in knowing things other people do not know. That's one of the ways that this system is being sold to you on the premise that there are marketing secrets. And it becomes a true premise if I know something that you don't know. To you, it is a secret. A very famous headline is simply, secrets of four champion golfers reveal. Golfers are the most manic people on earth about improving their golf games. Golfers will do anything. They will hike barefoot to the top of the mountain of Tibet to find the wise golfer who will tell them how to shave one stroke off their score. They will do anything to improve their golf games. And if a headline says secrets of four champion golfers, they want to know what those secrets are. So how could a local business use that headline? Any sporting goods store could use it. Any country club could use it. Could a restaurant use that headline? Sure, a restaurant could host golf or day and invite the golf pros from the local country club to be there free to promote their business. And they would give a little half hour lunchtime golf instruction seminar out in the parking lot. The restaurant runs it as a promotion to attract customers to their restaurant. That headline secrets of dot, dot, dot, dot, can apply to just about anything. Certainly you can use that headline in the beauty business, secrets of younger looking skin, secrets Hollywood actresses know for great hair. Another kind of headline starts with a hated problem you solve, instead of with a big benefit you offer. A successful example is, do you hate your computer? The copy that follows is, do you own a computer that won't do what you tell it to do? Are you afraid to buy a new computer even though you know you need one? Are you confused by computer babble? That headline is designed to cause immediate identification and affinity, if you will. And we talked about affinity earlier with anybody who opens that letter who does have problems with their computer. Another headline formula I call fortune telling. A really good prediction headline, for example, for a company selling burglar alarms in a sales letter going out to a neighborhood might be, at least one home within walking distance of yours will be robbed this year. I can give you an acid test for the headlines of most ads or sales letters. The test is based on the National Enquirer, which like it or not, has the widest largest circulation of any media. More people read the National Enquirer than read anything else. For decades, the Enquirer was home of some of the most profitable classified ads. Print media classified advertising has been drained away by Craigslist, eBay, and other online media, and the principles of print also migrate there. But a classified ad is a good place to learn how to do this. A classified ad is nothing more than a headline and a how-to instruction with all the copy taken out of the middle. Our test is to take the headline out of your ad, out of context, away from everything else, and run it as a classified ad in the National Enquirer with nothing but its headline and a how-to-respond instruction and ask ourselves, would anybody respond? So let's take a headline you might see on a typical local small businesses yellow pages ad, Valpak coupon, website, and other media. The headline is, Sam's Bowling Alley. That's the headline. So we put it in a National Enquirer and our classified ad reads like this, Sam's Bowling Alley, Box 3, Grand Central Station, New York, New York. How many letters are you going to get? None. There's no reason for anybody to write to you. So the ad fails, the headline fails, that's the National Enquirer test. Instead, Sam might come up with a headline for his Bowling Alley ad, 10 secret ways to add 100 points to your bowling score. Now if we run that in the National Enquirer with Write to Box 3, Grand Central Station, New York, New York, I promise you, we're going to get some mail. So that's a great headline for a Facebook ad or a Valpak coupon. Beyond the opening gambit, there are formulas for the full copy and copywriting of an ad, a sales letter, a video sales letter placed online, and other media. There are very sure-footed, reliable, easy-to-use formulas in my book, The Ultimate Sales Letter, Fourth Edition. There are formulas in another of my books, The No BS Guide to Powerful Presentations. Most of the examples in your Magnetic Marketing Toolkit are built with these formulas. One of the most common and one of the easiest formulas to learn is called Problem, Agitate, Solve. You set up the existence of a problem that your reader has, and you get them emotionally worked up about the effects of that problem on him. Then you whip out the answer to the problem that is going to solve it instantly and quickly and easily for him. When you learn the formulas, writing becomes easy. You may think, jeez, I have trouble writing a grocery list that makes any sense right now. But with these formulas, you become a good sales writer. You may not necessarily win a grammar award. You might not necessarily be able to write a novel, but you will be able to write great letters that sell and that get response just by following the formulas. I'll wrap up by talking with you about money. What Magnetic Marketing is really all about is financial control of your business and financial efficiency. It puts you more firmly in control of your attraction of new customers. It also enables you to attract better customers for your particular business. You have to understand that the customer is an asset to be acquired by investment and to be taken care of and maintained and that you have an opportunity to start with at value A and build all the way to better and bigger value B with each customer that you get and each customer that you have. For that to happen, you have to do as good of a job at marketing to your existing customers as you do at marketing to get new customers. Just as you acquire them with Magnetic Marketing, you can develop them, retain them, and sequentially sell to them with Magnetic Marketing. One of the most important keys to maximum customer value is in retention and repeat business. That is not an entitlement from excellent products and excellent service. It is also a result of excellent marketing. That includes continuing to market to them, not taking them for granted and communicating again and again in a magnetic manner. This requires frequently having and presenting a good answer to the question that is so important to us that it has become part of our vernacular. It is how we greet each other. The question is, what is new? I do not say, tell me everything that is the same as it was the last time I saw you three months ago. I am not interested in that. I am interested in what is new. If you do not have a good answer to what is new, they go looking for it somewhere else. A bored customer goes elsewhere. They forget about you. They do not talk about you. They do not buy from you. They are easily wooed by some competitor who comes along with something that is new and exciting. So how do you answer this question that is always on their minds? I have talked about that answer here, and it is shown to you in many of the toolkit examples. You answer this question by frequently and continually reinventing your business inside your business, by inventing new widgets and offering those new widgets through constant communication with your past and present customers, at least once a month, if not more often. They are hearing from you and they are getting a new widget offered to them. That will keep customers, keep customers interested and engaged, sell to them more frequently, and even get them to refer more often. Point is, your complete magnetic marketing system should incorporate what in direct marketing lingo is called front end and back end. Front end refers to outreach to attract and acquire new leads, new potential customers, and new customers. Back end means developing and retaining those customers, increasing the frequency of repeat business you do with them, cross selling different products and services, putting fresh offers in front of them frequently, staying interesting to them, so they stay with you and tell others about you. I hope what I have had to say here and what you learn and discover throughout the magnetic marketing system motivates you to work on and develop your own complete system. I know that we have given you the tools and the strategies to do exactly that. However, if you find that you need or want assistance with any of the ideas, strategies, or tools, or you'd like some hands-on assistance with some of your marketing problems or business challenges, the team at GKIC welcomes your call or email. There are a variety of ways that you can get support, coaching, even customized implementation from GKIC, the undisputed leader in direct marketing for all kinds of businesses. You have come to the right place. Congratulations! You've completed all three sessions. The methods and strategies laid out in these sessions have been proven again and again to literally revolutionize businesses and quickly make business owners much more skilled, effective, innovative, competitive, and efficient marketers. Make sure to take action on what you've discovered in these sessions. To make this super simple, we've provided a comprehensive action guide, as well as tons of swipe files and templates that you can use to build your own magnetic marketing campaigns and promotions. In addition, if you have any questions or could use additional help with anything you've encountered in this program, make sure to contact us at 1-800-871-0147. Or go to www.gkic.com. Thank you once again for listening to this GKIC program.
